name: Route 25: Donostia Cycling Loop
name_original: Monte Igeldo – circular desde San Sebastian por la Playa de la Concha y el Peine del Viento
description: Cycle a moderate 14.4-mile loop from San Sebastian, enjoying panoramic views from Monte Igeldo and passing Playa de la Concha and Peine del
region_intro: Touring cycling around Saint-Sébastien offers diverse landscapes, from rugged coastlines to green mountains. The city is situated at the mouth of the Urumea River, providing scenic riverside paths. An extensive network of over 65 kilometers of dedicated bike lanes, known as "bidegorri," makes the region highly accessible for cyclists. This area features a dramatic interplay of mountains like Igueldo and Jaizkibel, and renowned beaches such as La Concha Bay. Best touring cycling routes around Saint-Sébastien The most popular touring cycling route is Route 25: Donostia Cycling Loop, a 14.4 miles (23.2 km) moderate trail that takes 1 hour 33 minutes to complete. This route offers panoramic views from Mount Igueldo and passes along La Concha Beach. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Ermita de Guadalupe – Mount Jaizkibel loop from Intxaurrondo, a difficult 31.9 miles (51.4 km) path. This challenging route ascends Mount Jaizkibel, known for its demanding nature and rewarding views over the sea and mountains. Local touring cyclists also love the Jaizkibel Summit – Mount Jaizkibel loop from Anoeta, a 33.8 miles (54.4 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 3 hours 40 minutes. Touring cycling around Saint-Sébastien is defined by coastal promenades, challenging mountain ascents, and scenic riverside paths. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy urban rides to strenuous mountain challenges. The routes in Saint-Sébastien are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 430 reviews. More than 6,700 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Saint-Sébastien's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Saint-Sébastien?
A: There are over 100 touring cycling routes in the Saint-Sébastien region, offering a wide variety of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from more than 430 reviews.

Q: What types of terrain and difficulty levels can I expect for touring cycling in Saint-Sébastien?
A: Saint-Sébastien offers a diverse range of terrain, from flat coastal promenades and scenic riverside paths to challenging mountain ascents. You'll find routes suitable for all fitness levels, including 25 easy routes, 38 moderate routes, and 44 difficult routes, ensuring options for beginners and experienced cyclists alike.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Saint-Sébastien?
A: Yes, Saint-Sébastien is very accessible for cyclists of all levels, including families. The city boasts over 65 kilometers of dedicated bike lanes, known as "bidegorri," which are ideal for leisurely urban rides. Routes along the urban beaches like La Concha Bay, Ondarreta Beach, and Zurriola Beach offer flat, scenic rides perfect for families.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass by significant landmarks and natural features. You can cycle past the elegant &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/567533" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Concha Beach&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5577591" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Sebastián City Hall&lt;/a&gt;, and the modern &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/567551" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Kursaal Bridge&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, routes often include ascents to mountains like Igueldo or Ulia, offering breathtaking vistas of the Cantabrian Sea and the city.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the region?
A: Yes, many routes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1065745951/route-25-donostia-cycling-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route 25: Donostia Cycling Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate route that offers panoramic views from Mount Igueldo and passes along La Concha Beach. For a more challenging option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40394851" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaizkibel Summit – Mount Jaizkibel loop from Anoeta&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Saint-Sébastien?
A: Saint-Sébastien's dynamic coastline and Atlantic air make it enjoyable for cycling through much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds. While the region is known for its green landscapes, which means some rain is possible, the mild climate often allows for cycling even in winter, especially on coastal and urban paths.

Q: How accessible are the touring cycling routes by public transport?
A: Saint-Sébastien has good public transport links, and many routes start or pass through urban areas, making them accessible. The city's extensive "bidegorri" network connects various neighborhoods and attractions, often allowing you to reach starting points easily. For routes further afield, local bus services might be an option, though checking specific bike transport policies is advisable.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes in Saint-Sébastien?
A: Parking is available in and around Saint-Sébastien, particularly in urban areas and near popular starting points for routes. Many routes begin in the city center or accessible neighborhoods. For routes venturing into the mountains or rural areas, look for designated parking facilities near trailheads or in nearby villages. It's always a good idea to check specific parking options for your chosen route beforehand.

Q: What makes Saint-Sébastien a unique destination for touring cyclists?
A: Saint-Sébastien stands out for its unique blend of stunning coastal scenery, challenging mountain ascents, and rich cultural experiences. Cyclists can enjoy the elegance of urban beaches, explore historic Old Town streets, and then quickly transition to demanding climbs on mountains like Igueldo or Jaizkibel, all while experiencing Basque culture and gastronomy.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners?
A: Absolutely. Saint-Sébastien offers 25 easy touring cycling routes. A great option for beginners is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38296788" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Kursaal Bridge – La Concha Beach loop from Santa Klara uhartea - Isla Santa Clara&lt;/a&gt;, which is a relatively flat route perfect for a relaxed ride along the iconic bay and city sights.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, Saint-Sébastien and its surrounding areas are well-equipped with places to stop. Urban routes will take you past numerous cafes, pintxos bars, and restaurants, especially in the Old Town and along the promenades. Even on more rural routes, you'll often find charming villages or traditional farmhouses (baserris) that might offer refreshments, particularly in areas known for Basque cider culture like Astigarraga.
tips:
Glamorous art deco style bridge spanning the Urumea River.
https://turismo.euskadi.eus/es/patrimonio-cultural/puente-de-la-zurriola/aa30-12375/es/
Formal park in front of the baroque city hall building. The excellent free municipal library is on the corner.

La Concha Bay is the heart of San Sebastian. The beach is a hallmark of the city and the ideal place to start a walk that will take you through its charming streets with tapas bars. For decades it has become an icon of the city and accumulates visitors who avidly photograph it.
Beautiful, delicate metal works of art right next to the rushing and foaming sea.

Old signal tower located on top of Mount Igueldo. The 18 meter high stone tower, built in 1855, is a testament to the region's maritime history. From the lighthouse you can enjoy impressive views of the coastal landscape and the city of San Sebastián. In addition, it is an ideal place to enjoy a beautiful sunset.
Up here there is also a small amusement park for children

The climb to Igedo is not easy, but it does have its rewards. These fabulous views to the west of Donostia-San Sebastián, the church of the Diocesan Seminary and, in the distance, the Basque hills will leave you speechless.
The Morlans tunnel is the protagonist of a practically underground walk. It holds the award of being the longest urban bicycle gallery in Europe.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Monte Igeldo circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 23.2 kilometers long with an elevation gain of about 337 meters. For touring cyclists, it typically takes around 1 hour and 33 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: The Monte Igeldo circular route is generally considered a moderate bike ride. While there is an initial steep climb with hairpin bends up Monte Igeldo, the gradients become much gentler afterward. If you have good basic fitness, you should find it manageable, but beginners might find the initial ascent challenging.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Monte Igeldo cycling route?
faqs.answer: The main cycling route is predominantly on paved surfaces, making it suitable for touring bikes. The initial climb up Monte Igeldo involves a series of hairpin bends, but after that, the road conditions are generally very good with gentler gradients. You'll cycle through a mix of urban areas, coastal paths, and sections with forests and meadows.

faqs.question: What are the main landmarks and highlights I'll see on this route?
faqs.answer: You'll pass by several iconic spots including the Kursaal Bridge, San Sebastián City Hall, and the beautiful La Concha Bay. A major highlight is the Peine del Viento sculptures. As you ascend Monte Igeldo, you'll reach the Monte Igueldo Lighthouse and be treated to incredible panoramic views from the View of La Concha Bay from Monte Igueldo and the View of San Sebastián. The route also includes the Morlans Tunnel.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Monte Igeldo route?
faqs.answer: The route is enjoyable for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds. Summer weekends can be busy, especially on the initial climb. Clear days are best for enjoying the extensive panoramic views of San Sebastián Bay and the coast, which can even stretch to Biarritz.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Monte Igeldo cycling route?
faqs.answer: As the route starts and ends in San Sebastián, you can look for public parking garages within the city. Many cyclists choose to start near the La Concha Bay or the Peine del Viento area, where you might find street parking or nearby paid lots. Consider parking slightly outside the busiest city center to avoid congestion, especially on weekends.

faqs.question: How can I get to the starting point of the route using public transport?
faqs.answer: San Sebastián has a good public transport network. You can reach the starting points near La Concha Bay or the Peine del Viento by local bus services. If you're coming from further afield, San Sebastián's train and bus stations are well-connected, and you can cycle from there to the route's beginning.

faqs.question: Is the Monte Igeldo cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in outdoor spaces in San Sebastián, this is primarily a touring bicycle route on paved roads and shared paths. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it is comfortable with cycling alongside or in a trailer, and always keep it on a leash, especially in busy areas and near traffic. Be mindful of other cyclists and pedestrians.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It uses public roads and paths. However, if you choose to visit the historic amusement park at the summit of Monte Igeldo, there will be an entrance fee for the park itself or the funicular ride.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, being a circular route starting and ending in San Sebastián, you'll find numerous cafes, restaurants, and bars in the city center and along the La Concha Bay. There are also establishments near the summit of Monte Igeldo, offering refreshments with stunning views.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths or trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, parts of this route coincide with other well-known paths. Notably, sections of the route, particularly on Monte Igeldo, align with the Northern Pilgrim's Way to Santiago de Compostela (Northern Route (Basque Country)). You'll also find it intersects with local cycling paths like Bidegorri and Ruta con prioridad peatonal, and passes through the Morlans Tunnel.

faqs.question: Are there any specific tips for cycling the initial climb up Monte Igeldo?
faqs.answer: The first few kilometers of the climb up Monte Igeldo from San Sebastián involve a series of hairpin bends and can be quite busy, especially on summer weekends. It's advisable to start earlier in the day to avoid peak traffic. Take your time on the ascent, as the gradients are steepest here, and save some energy for the rest of the scenic ride.

waypoints.name: View of La Concha Bay from Monte Igueldo
